explanations.35: BEGIN is a special Perl block that runs once at the start—it's where we set up variables before processing any lines.
explanations.42: The expression ($buf eq "") checks if the buffer is empty; if it is, we don't add the separator, otherwise we do—this keeps us from starting with extra characters.
explanations.64: We're creating a string of 150 spaces to act as invisible padding, which pushes the description far to the right so it doesn't clutter the left side of the display.
explanations.65: The printf statement uses format specifiers like %-20s (left-align in 20 spaces) to create columnar output that fzf will later split on the ###### delimiter.
explanations.70: The --delimiter parameter tells fzf to treat ##### (from the Perl output) as the split point, so it knows which part is the searchable list and which is the preview data.
explanations.77: The --preview parameter runs a Perl command on field {2} (everything after the #####) when you highlight an item—that's how the right pane gets populated.
explanations.91: ssh -G is a special command that outputs all the computed SSH settings for an alias (like the actual hostname and username), rather than connecting.
explanations.94: We use head -n 1 to grab only the first identity file because ssh -G can output multiple keys, but we just need one.
explanations.97: The \e codes are ANSI escape sequences—\e[1;36m means bold cyan text, and \e[0m resets the formatting back to normal.
